meta.title: 3.8 Checkpoint
meta.description: Design the data layer for a social media platform with 100M users.

blocks.content:
## Level 3 Checkpoint

Apply everything from the data layer. Answer without looking back at the modules.

blocks.question: You are building a social media platform. User profiles are read 1000x more than they are updated. The profile page shows: name, bio, follower count, recent posts, and mutual friends. Design the data model and choose your databases.
blocks.answer:
**Databases:** (1) PostgreSQL for user accounts (ACID, authentication data). (2) Redis for follower counts and recent post IDs (pre-computed, sub-ms reads). (3) Neo4j or a graph table for follower relationships and mutual friends. (4) MongoDB or PostgreSQL for posts (document or row per post with embedded author name for denormalization).

**Data model:** Denormalize heavily since the read:write ratio is 1000:1. Store follower_count directly on the user profile (update on follow/unfollow). Pre-compute 'recent posts' as a list of post IDs in Redis. Mutual friends require a graph query — cache the result with a short TTL.

**Consistency:** Use CDC from PostgreSQL to keep Redis and search indexes updated. Follower counts can be eventually consistent (showing 10,001 vs 10,002 followers does not matter). User profile updates should have read-your-writes consistency.

blocks.question: Your users table has 100M rows sharded by user_id (hash partitioning). A new feature requires finding all users in a specific city. How do you support this query efficiently?
blocks.answer: **You cannot efficiently query by city across hash-partitioned shards** — it requires a scatter-gather across all shards. Solutions: (1) **Secondary index service:** Maintain a city → user_id mapping in Elasticsearch or a separate database. Query Elasticsearch for user IDs in the city, then fetch user details from the relevant shards. (2) **Materialized view:** Create a denormalized table partitioned by city (or a city-based lookup table). Keep it updated via CDC. (3) **Dual-shard:** If city-based queries are frequent, consider a second copy of the data sharded by city (expensive but fast). The right solution depends on query frequency and acceptable staleness.

blocks.title: Design a sharding strategy for an orders table in an e-commerce platform. The table grows by 10M rows/day. Common queries: (1) Get orders by customer_id, (2) Get orders by order_id, (3) Get recent orders by product_id, (4) Monthly sales report. Which queries can be served from a single shard, and which require scatter-gather?
blocks.solution: **Shard key: customer_id** — this supports query (1) from a single shard and is the most common access pattern (users viewing their orders). Query (2) can be served efficiently if order_id embeds the customer_id (or a lookup table maps order_id to customer_id/shard). Query (3) requires scatter-gather across all shards OR a secondary index (Elasticsearch indexed by product_id). Query (4) is an analytics query — route it to a read replica or analytics database (ClickHouse/BigQuery) populated via CDC, not the transactional shards. Key insight: optimize your shard key for the most frequent query pattern and use secondary systems for other patterns.

blocks.content:
## Ready for Level 4?

If you can choose databases, design schemas for access patterns, plan replication and sharding, and handle cross-shard queries, you are ready. Level 4 covers the speed layer — caching strategies that make systems feel instant.
